segments.s619-g1: وزان روی is a short adverbial phrase meaning "from that time / on that account": وزان (a fused form of و + زان) marks continuation/from then on, and روی here functions adverbially (literally "on/for that [account]"). بهرام is the subject (proper name). بیدار is an adjective meaning "awake/alert" and بود is the past copula "was"; together بیدار بود = "was alert." Word order is adverbial phrase → subject → predicate (typical for epic Persian).
segments.s619-g2: سپه را is marked with the object/topic marker را (here topicalizing the army as the thing being kept/protected). ز دشمن is a prepositional phrase "from (or against) the enemy" using the poetic preposition ز. نگهدار is a predicate noun "guardian/protector," and بود is the past copula "was," so نگهدار بود = "was the guardian/protector." The subject (Bahram) is understood from the previous segment.
segments.s619-g3: شب و روز is an adverbial phrase "night and day." کارآگهان (an adverbial/participial adjective) means "vigilant/aware", and داشتی is a poetic past form of داشتن used here as a past habitual/continuous copula meaning "kept/maintained" (in epic Persian, -ی on داشت often appears where modern Persian has داشت or داشتِ). The clause therefore means "he kept (himself/was) vigilant night and day."
segments.s619-g4: سپه را again topicalizes the army. ز دشمن "from/against the enemy" marks source/agent of danger. نهان is an adjective/adverb meaning "hidden/secretly," and داشتی (as above) is the poetic past of داشتن meaning "kept"—here forming the verbal phrase نهان داشتی "kept hidden." Word order: object/topic → prepositional phrase → adjective + verbal auxiliary.
segments.s619-g5: چو is the subordinating conjunction "when." آگهی "news/information" is the subject of آمد ("arrived/was brought"). به + بهرامشاه marks the indirect object/recipient: "news came to Bahramshah." The verb آمد is simple past "came/arrived."
segments.s619-g6: که introduces the content of the news: a nominal clause. خاقان (Khaghan) is the subject; به مروست uses the poetic preposition به with a place name to indicate location "is at Marv" (the copula 'است' is omitted, as is common). و چندان سپاه follows as a trailing descriptive phrase "and so many troops/so large an army," where چندان quantifies سپاه. The sentence fragment functions as the reported content of the earlier آمد.
segments.s619-g7: بیاورد is a poetical past/preterite form meaning "he brought/led" (third-person singular). لشکر is the direct object "army." ز here means "from/of" (indicating origin or source) and links to the name آذر گشسپ (Azar Goshasp), a compound name: either "Goshasp of Azar" or a two-part name. Word order is verb → object → source/agent.
segments.s619-g8: همه بی‌بنه is an adjectival phrase modifying the troops: همه "all/everyone" + بی‌بنه "without means/ill-equipped" (i.e., lacking equipment). هر یکی is a distributive phrase "each one," and با دو اسپ "with two steeds/horses" gives the accompaniment for each man. The clause therefore says all were ill-equipped, each with two horses.
segments.s619-g57: ز is the poetic preposition 'from / because of' introducing the circumstance 'ز پیروزی چین' (from the victory over China). چین here is a place-name (China) in genitive with پیروزی. چو is the temporal conjunction 'when', and سربر فراخت is a compacted poetic clause meaning 'he raised his head' (سربر = 'head‑high / with head raised' as an adverbial phrase; فراخت is the past action 'raised/spread' (3rd person) used here to form the temporal clause). Word order is conventional poetic Persian: prepositional phrase + temporal چو + verb phrase.
segments.s619-g58: همه کامگاری is a noun phrase 'all / every success(s)' (همه before the noun intensifies totality). ز یزدان marks the source 'from God', and شناخت here functions as the past action 'he recognized / perceived' (3rd person) with the subject implied from context; the clause means 'he perceived that all success was from God.'
segments.s619-g59: کجا is the interrogative 'where' (a rhetorical question here). داد is used as the noun 'justice' or 'judgment' rather than the verb 'gave', and بر marks the domain or target 'with respect to / over' نیک و بد ('good and bad'). دستگاه is 'system / ordering / régime' — together the phrase asks where justice is in the ordering of good and evil.
segments.s619-g60: که is the relative/linking particle 'that / for / who' connecting the rhetorical question to a statement about the divine. دارنده is the agent noun/participle 'possessor / holder'. آفتابست و ماه names the heavenly bodies 'the sun and the moon' (the copular -ست is attached to آفتاب in a compact, poetic way); the whole segment identifies the possessor as the one who holds the sun and the moon (i.e., God).
segments.s619-g41: چنان شد is a fixed opening meaning "it became so" (چنان + شد, here شد = past 'became'). ز خون خاک آوردگاه is a prepositional/nominal phrase describing cause and location: ز + خون = "with/from blood" modifying خاک آوردگاه (آوردگاه = "battlefield"). Word order places the resultive verb before the descriptive noun phrase for a compact poetic effect.
segments.s619-g42: که introduces the clause giving the resulting image "that...". گفتی همی here is an impersonal, rhetorical formula roughly "one might say" or "it was as if people said" (گفتی used idiomatically rather than a literal 'you said'); همی is an old adverbial intensifier. تیربارد is a compound noun meaning literally "arrow‑shower/arrow‑rain"; ز ماه is a poetic locative/simile "from the moon" (i.e. as if arrows came from the moon).
segments.s619-g43: چو introduces a temporal clause here, "when." سیصد تن is an understood numeral phrase (three hundred people); تن = "person" (here pluralized by context). از نامداران چین identifies origin/group: از = "of/from," نامداران = "the renowned ones," چین = "China" (the country). The whole phrase means "when three hundred of China's famed men..."
segments.s619-g44: Two past verbs in sequence describe the actions: گرفتند (they seized/captured) و بستند (and they bound/tied). The object (the captured men) is contextually understood. بر پشت زین is a prepositional phrase 'on the back of the saddle' (بر = "on", پشت = "back/body", زین = "saddle").
segments.s619-g45: چو again = "when." خاقان چینی is the compound subject 'the Chinese khagan.' گرفتار شد is a passive/periphrastic construction 'became captured' (گرفتار = 'captured/entangled' + شد = past 'became'), i.e. 'was captured.'
segments.s619-g46: ازان خواب is an ablative phrase 'from that sleep' (ازان = 'from that/of that', خواب = 'sleep'). آنگاه بیدار شد is a tense/aspect sequence: آنگاه = 'then/afterwards', بیدار شد = 'became awake' (brought about by شد), so the clause means 'then he woke.'
segments.s619-g47: سپهبد ز کشمیهن آمد به مرو: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed/general.' ز + کشمیهن can indicate origin or association ('from/of Kushmihan' or 'of the enemy of the land' depending on sense); آمد is the simple past 'came,' and به مرو marks the destination 'to Merv.' The clause therefore states the general arrived at Merv from/associated with کشمیهن.
segments.s619-g48: شد here is resultative/past 'became.' از تاختن چارپایان is a noun phrase 'from the galloping/charging of the four‑footed ones' (چارپایان = mounts/horses), where از marks the source/agent of the resulting effect. چو غرو is a simile/comparative 'like/as dusk' (چو = 'as/like', غرو = 'dusk/twilight'), so the clause says the galloping's effect 'became like dusk' (a poetic way to describe its sound or the onset of evening).
segments.s619-g17: به introduces a directional/adverbial phrase here meaning 'into/towards'; کوه و بیابان are coordinated nouns 'mountains and wilderness'; بی‌راه is an adverbial/ adjectival phrase 'off the road/astray' that modifies رفت. رفت is the simple past 'went'. Word order is typical Persian: adverbial phrase + verb.
segments.s619-g18: به‌روز and به شب‌گاه are adverbial prepositional phrases 'by day' / 'in the night-time' (به + time-word). بی‌گاه is an adjectival/adverbial compound meaning 'untimely / at inopportune hours'. رفت repeats the past-tense verb 'went'. The three time-phrases are coordinated to stress movement at all hours.
segments.s619-g19: به‌روز ('by day') is an adverbial time phrase; اندرون 'inside/within' follows it, forming 'by day, inside'. دیده‌بان is the direct object 'a watchman/sentinel'; داشتی is the past-tense 'had' (narrative past, here rendered 'he had'). The order is adverbial → place → object → verb.
segments.s619-g20: به here functions again as a temporal/locative marker 'at/during'; تیره qualifies شبان as 'dark/ominous', so تیره شبان = 'the dark nights' (an archaic plural use of شب). پاسبان is the object 'guard/watch'; داشتی is again 'he had'. Note: شبان is used here poetically as 'nights' (archaic), not the literal 'shepherd'.
segments.s619-g21: بدین‌سان is a discourse adverb 'thus/so'; بیامد is the past 'he came'; به نزدیک is a locative phrase 'to/near'; مرو is the place-name Merv. The clause is a straightforward narrative line: 'Thus he came near Merv.'
segments.s619-g22: نپرد is a negated verbal form from پرید here meaning 'did not fly/flee' (narrative negative). بدان گونه is a frozen adverbial phrase 'in that manner' (ب as prepositional prefix + دان 'that' + گونه 'manner'). پران is an adjectival/participial form 'winged' or 'flying', and تذرو is a verb/noun meaning 'scatter / disperse' that follows to describe a fluttering, scattering motion. Together they describe that he did not flee in that winged, scattering way.
segments.s619-g23: نوندی (Navand) here functions as a proper name or a named messenger/mode — treated as the subject 'Navand came'. بیامد is 'came'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; کارآگهان is a plural/collective meaning 'the informed ones / those aware' (i.e., informants or scouts) and is the source from which Navand came.
segments.s619-g24: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause 'for/that'; خاقان is 'the khagan / supreme ruler' (subject); شب و روز is a standard phrase 'night and day'; بی‌اندهان is an intensifying adjective/adverb meaning 'without ceasing / incessantly / ever (watchful)' in context — it qualifies the khagan's constant vigilance. The whole clause explains the reason or circumstance.
segments.s619-g25: به here marks the instrumental/causal sense 'by' (how something is done). تدبیر is an abstract noun 'device/stratagem' and is the thing attributed to the actor; نخچیر کشمیهن is a poetic nominal group in which کشمیهن (the homeland‑destroyer / enemy of the country) qualifies نخچیر (the plunder/booty or plunderer) — the linking ezâfe is omitted in poetry — and the whole phrase is the subject/predicate complement followed by the copula است.
segments.s619-g26: که introduces an explanatory subordinate clause 'for/because'. دستورش (دستور + possessive -ش) means 'his command/plan'. ازکهل is an archaic locution 'from/of old/ancient' giving the origin of that command, and اهریمنست is the predicate 'is Ahrimanic / is demonic' (copula attached). Together the clause explains the origin and nature of the stratagem.
segments.s619-g27: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بهرام is the proper name (subject). بشنید is simple past 'heard'. زان ('from that') points back to the preceding news, and شاد شد is a two‑word predicate 'became glad' (شاد = 'happy', شد = past of شدن 'to become').
segments.s619-g28: همه رنجها = 'all the sufferings' (subject). بر دلش = locative phrase 'upon his heart' (بر = 'on/upon', دل + -ش = 'his heart'). باد شد is a poetic idiom: باد ('wind') + شد ('became') = 'were blown away' (i.e. the troubles vanished or were scattered).
segments.s619-g29: برآسود is a stative verb/adjective meaning 'was at rest/ceased to trouble'. روزی is the temporal adverb 'one day' (poetic usage). بدان رزمگاه = به + آن + رزمگاه 'to/at that battlefield' (the poet often contracts or drops the explicit ezâfe; ب + دان functions as 'to/at that'). Together: 'one day that battlefield was at rest.'
segments.s619-g30: چو is 'when'. آسوده‌تر is the comparative 'more at ease/more relieved'. گشت is past 'became'. شاه و سپاه (king and army) are the coordinated subjects that follow the verb, a normal poetic word order inversion: 'when the king and army became more relieved.'
segments.s619-g31: به marks direction 'to/upon' and کشمیهن is the addressee/target ('the homeland‑destroyer' or enemy). آمد is simple past 'came/arrived'. به هنگام روز is a temporal phrase 'at the time of day / at daybreak' (به + هنگام + روز). The order places the destination first then the time phrase.
segments.s619-g32: که introduces the subordinate clause (here temporal/relative 'when/which'). برزد is an archaic verb meaning here 'rose/appeared/struck upward' (poetic usage). سر = 'head'; از کوه = 'from the mountain'; گیتی فروز is a compound epithet 'world‑illumining' or 'brightening the world' (گیتی = world, فروز = bright/illuminating). The clause says that when he lifted/raised his head from the mountain the world was lit/glorious.
segments.s619-g33: همه گوش پرناله بوق شد is a compact poetic clause: همه گوش ('every ear') is the subject phrase, پرناله ('full of lament') modifies گوش and describes the state, and بوق شد ('became a horn') is the predicate with شد as the past/resultive copula 'became'. The image is metaphorical: ears are said to 'become a horn' because they are filled with loud lamentation. Word order is Subject–Modifier–Predicate, typical of Persian poetic compression.
segments.s619-g34: همه چشم پر رنگ منجوق شد: همه چشم ('every eye') is the subject, پر ('full (of)') is an adjectival prepositive element that takes the following noun as the content (رنگ منجوق = 'the color/gleam of beads'), and شد is the copula 'became'. The clause means the eyes were filled with (or took on) the gleam/colour of beads — a visual metaphor for sparkling or jewel-like eyes.
segments.s619-g35: دهاده برآمد ز نخچیرگاه: دهاده ('many/several' or 'a large number') is the subject (an indefinite plural idea), برآمد is the past 'arose/issued forth', and ز + نخچیرگاه gives the source 'from the hunting-ground'. The verb is intransitive here: 'many (voices/people) came out from the hunting-ground.'
segments.s619-g36: پرآواز شد گوش شاه و سپاه: پرآواز ('loud-voiced/sonorous') is a predicate adjective built into a nominal phrase; شد is the copula 'became'; گوش شاه و سپاه ('the ear(s) of the king and the army') is the affected locus. Literally, 'the ears of the king and the host became sonorous' — i.e. their ears were filled with loud sound.
segments.s619-g37: بدرید از آواز گوش هژبر: بدرید is the past-form of دریدن ('to tear'), here describing a tearing/rending experienced by هژبر (Hajbar). The construction uses از ('from/by') with آواز ('the sound') to mark the cause/agent — 'torn by the sound' — so although بدرید is formally active, the sense is passive/resultive ('was torn'). Word order is verb – agent/ablative – patient, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s619-g38: تو گفتی همی ژاله بارد ز ابر: تو گفتی is the 2nd-person past 'you said/used to say'; همی is a habitual/continuous particle (here indicating repeated or customary speech); ژاله بارد ز ابر is the quoted content ('dew/teardrops fall from the cloud'), where بارد is the classical present/optative form of باریدن ('to fall/rain'). Grammatically: 'you would say (habitually), "dew falls from the cloud."' Note: the supplied lexical senses for گفتی and بارد did not include the verbal readings, so they are marked below as unmatched.
segments.s619-g39: چو خاقان ز نخچیر بیدار شد: چو here means 'when'; خاقان is the subject ('the khagan'); ز نخچیر gives the circumstance/source ('from the hunt'); بیدار شد is the past/resultive phrase 'became awake/was roused'. The clause thus means 'when the khagan was awakened from the hunt'.
segments.s619-g40: به دست خزروان گرفتار شد: this is a passive-style phrasing where به دست + agent indicates 'into the hands of' (i.e. 'by' or 'at the power of') and گرفتار شد ('became captured/ensnared') is the predicate. Together: 'he was taken/captured by the kings.' The verb شد is the resultive copula forming the passive/result state.
segments.s619-g49: This is a locative clause: به مرو اندر is a two-part locative ('into/at Merv'); از چینیان marks the group ('of/from the Chinese'); کس نماند is a negated past-state verb phrase meaning 'no one remained'. Word order is topic (place + group) then predicate. 'نماند' is past-tense/resultant sense 'was left'.
segments.s619-g50: This clause contains two parts: بکشتند (past plural 'they killed') as the main verb, then وز جنگیان بس نماند expresses the result/continuation — 'and of the warriors not even one remained'. Here وز functions as a classical contraction (= و از) meaning 'and of/from' rather than the listed literal senses.
segments.s619-g51: هرانکس 'whoever' is the indefinite relative subject; کزیشان ('از ایشان' fused) links it to 'of them'/'from among them'; گریزان is a participial/adjectival form 'fleeing'; برفت is an archaic past verb 'he went/left' (here: 'fled away'). Note the compact classical prose order: indefinite + relative + participle + verb.
segments.s619-g52: پس‌اندر همی تاخت is a temporal/sequential clause 'after that he kept rushing/rode on' — همی gives an imperfective/continuous flavour to the past verb تاخت ('galloped/rushed'); پس‌اندر = 'afterwards/thereupon'. بهرام تفت is a proper-name phrase (Bahrām with an epithet); the epithet تفت here functions as part of the name.
segments.s619-g53: برین‌سان همی‌راند فرسنگ سی: برین‌سان is an adverbial/phrase meaning 'in that manner' or 'thus'; همی‌راند is presentive/continuous past 'he kept driving/riding'; فرسنگ is the unit 'farsang/parasang' and combines with سی ('thirty') following it. The clause expresses duration/distance of the riding.
segments.s619-g54: پس پشت او قارن پارسی = 'behind him (was) Qaran the Persian.' پس (here 'after') + پشت ('rear/behind') gives the locative 'at his back'; او is the pronoun 'him'; the noun phrase قارن پارسی names the person at his rear (پارسی 'the Persian' is an epithet). Word order places the locative phrase before the nominal subject.
segments.s619-g55: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; برگشت و آمد are two consecutive past verbs 'he turned back and came' (classical style allows simple coordination of actions); به + نخچیرگاه marks direction/location 'to the hunting-ground'.
segments.s619-g56: ببخشید is the past tense meaning 'he bestowed/gave' (here transitive: 'gave gifts'); چیز کسان here = 'things to people' (i.e., gifts to certain persons); بر سپاه indicates the recipients' group 'to/among the army/host' (بر used here with dative-like force). The clause as a whole says he distributed gifts to members of his army.
segments.s619-g9: This is a simple noun list naming garments: قبا (coat) then جوشن (cuirass/armor) linked by و (and) to ترگ رومی کلاه, a compound where رومی is an adjective modifying ترگ and کلاه names a headpiece. Word order is noun–noun–و–modifier–noun; the items are coordinated and form the direct description of what they wore.
segments.s619-g10: شب و روز (night and day) is a fixed phrase; چون introduces a simile 'like', باد is the image 'wind', and تازان is an attributive participial/adjectival form describing their manner ('fierce/pressing' here, i.e. rushing). به راه is a prepositional phrase giving direction ('on/toward the road'). The cadence places time first (day and night) and then the manner (like the wind) + motion.
segments.s619-g11: همی is a frequentative/continuous adverb ('kept' / 'still'), تاخت is a past-tense verb ('rushed/galloped') agreeing with لشکر (the army/host). چو functions as a simile marker 'like/as', followed by از کوه سیل ('a flood from the mountain') as the image: 'the host rushed on like a flood from the mountain.'
segments.s619-g12: به آمل is a directional prepositional phrase ('toward/to Amol'), with گذشت the past verb 'passed'. از در اردبیل is a route/instrument phrase ('through/from the gate of Ardabil'); در here means 'gate'. The clause gives the destination and the route taken.
segments.s619-g13: ز (poetic from) marks origin: 'from Amol'. بیامد is the past tense 'he came' and به گرگان gives the destination. The clause ends with کشید, here used as a past verb in a transitive/causative sense ('brought' / 'inflicted'); in the poem the verb is repeated for emphasis in the next segment (poetic parallelism).
segments.s619-g14: همی again marks continuous/iterative aspect. درد و رنج is a coordinated noun phrase 'pain and suffering'; بزرگان ('the great ones') is the affected party. کشید here repeats the verb and functions as 'he inflicted/caused' (an older/poetic use of کشیدن meaning to bring or to cause suffering). The sequence is typical epic parallelism: action—result—repeated verb for emphasis.
segments.s619-g15: ز marks origin ('from Gorgan'), بیامد is the past 'came', and به شهر نسا is the destination phrase ('to the city Nisa'). Word order is origin – verb – destination, a common poetic order in Persian.
segments.s619-g16: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain' (introducing a person), رهنمون is the noun 'guide'. پیش here means 'before/in front' (i.e., going ahead), and پر + کیمیا form 'full of kimia' — پر is the adjectival 'full (of)' and کیمیا here is understood as an elixir/secret substance. Together the phrase describes 'a certain guide who went ahead, full of the elixir.'

sentences.s619-s1: From that time Bahram was alert; he served as the army's guardian against the enemy.
sentences.s619-s2: Day and night he kept watch; he kept the army hidden from the enemy.
sentences.s619-s3: When news reached Bahramshah that the Khaghan was at Marv with so large an army,
sentences.s619-s4: Goshasp (of Azar) brought an army; they were all poorly equipped—each man with two steeds.
sentences.s619-s29: When he lifted his head after the victory over China, he recognized that all success comes from God.
sentences.s619-s30: Where then is justice in the ordering of good and evil, since He is the possessor of the sun and the moon?
sentences.s619-s21: The battlefield's earth became so stained with blood that one would say arrows were raining down from the moon.
sentences.s619-s22: When three hundred of China's renowned men were captured, they seized them and bound them on the backs of saddles.
sentences.s619-s23: When the Chinese khagan was captured, he then woke from that sleep.
sentences.s619-s24: The spahbed (general) came from Kushmihan to Merv; at the galloping of the mounts the sound became like dusk.
sentences.s619-s9: He went off the road into the mountains and the wilderness; he went by day, and by night-time, and at untimely hours.
sentences.s619-s10: By day he had a sentinel inside; at the dark nights he had guards.
sentences.s619-s11: Thus he came near Merv; he did not fly off in that manner — winged and scattering.
sentences.s619-s12: Navand came from the informants, for the khagan was ever watchful day and night.
sentences.s619-s13: It is by the stratagem of the homeland‑destroyer (the plunderer), for his command is from ancient Ahriman.
sentences.s619-s14: When Bahram heard that, he became glad; all his troubles were blown from his heart.
sentences.s619-s15: One day that battlefield was at rest, when the king and the army became more at ease.
sentences.s619-s16: He came upon the homeland‑destroyer at the time of day; when he raised his head above the mountain the world shone.
sentences.s619-s17: All ears became like horns full of lament; all eyes became filled with the color of beads.
sentences.s619-s18: A large throng issued forth from the hunting-ground; the ears of the king and the army became resonant.
sentences.s619-s19: Hajbar's ear was torn by the sound; you kept saying, 'Dew falls from the cloud.'
sentences.s619-s20: When the khagan was roused from the hunt, he was seized by the kings.
sentences.s619-s25: In Merv there remained no one of the Chinese; they killed them, and of the fighting-men not a single one was left.
sentences.s619-s26: Whoever of them fled, Bahram the Scorcher rode after (him).
sentences.s619-s27: He kept riding like that for thirty parasangs; at his rear was Qaran the Persian.
sentences.s619-s28: When he returned and came to the hunting-ground, he gave gifts to some people of the host.
sentences.s619-s5: They wore coats, cuirasses, and Roman ragged caps; day and night, like the wind, they rushed along the road.
sentences.s619-s6: The army kept rushing like a flood down from the mountain; it passed to Amol through the gate of Ardabil.
sentences.s619-s7: From Amol he came to Gorgan; he brought pain and suffering and afflicted the great.
sentences.s619-s8: From Gorgan he came to the city of Nisa; a certain guide went ahead, full of the elixir.
